Once that move is made, every problem becomes a problem of estimating a number, every decision becomes an expected-value calculation, and the option "we do not know, and cannot put a number on it" disappears from the vocabulary of the discipline. The consequence is systematic overconfidence: institutions build risk models that report precise probabilities for events that are genuinely unknowable, then act as if the precision were real. Distinguish this from the bare risk/uncertainty distinction itself - the value of this note is the historical claim that the discipline consciously eliminated Knightian uncertainty, and the identification of that elimination as causally implicated in real-world catastrophe.

## Evidence

Kay & King: "in the last two decades probabilistic reasoning has almost completely dominated the description and analysis of decision-making under uncertainty." And on the historical defeat: "Keynes and Knight lost the battle." The diffusion claim - that the turn propagated from economics into statistics, sociology, psychology, and law - documents the breadth of the conceptual annexation. As a history-of-ideas claim about a documented intellectual movement, acceptance is established; the causal link to 2008 is the authors' interpretive thesis.

nodes.id: Books/radical-uncertainty-kay-king/The Small-World Large-World Distinction.md
nodes.title: The Small-World Large-World Distinction
nodes.content:
# The Small-World Large-World Distinction

**Source**: John Kay & Mervyn King, *Radical Uncertainty: Decision-Making Beyond the Numbers* (W. W. Norton, 2020) - Ch. 7
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/radical-uncertainty-kay-king

## Core Insight

Optimisation and subjective-expected-utility maximisation presuppose a CLOSED, fully-enumerated problem space - what Leonard Savage called a "small world," in which every relevant state and option is already listed. Most consequential decisions occur in a "large world" of unknown unknowns, where the option set itself is incomplete. There, no optimum can be computed over a space you cannot specify; the only way to resolve the residual is to act and find out.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters

The mathematics of expected-utility maximisation requires an exhaustive list of states of the world and their consequences. That requirement is satisfiable only in a small world - a board game, a casino bet, an actuarial table. In a large world, the consequential gap is not missing probabilities over known outcomes but missing outcomes: the engineering "unk-unks" you have not imagined and therefore cannot have enumerated. Optimising over an incomplete option set produces an answer that is precise about the wrong problem.

The decisive tell is that Savage himself regarded extending his axioms beyond small worlds as "preposterous" / unreasonable - the originator of the formal apparatus drew the boundary that later users ignored. Kay & King's practical corollary is that in a large world the resolution mechanism is empirical, not computational: you act, observe, and revise the option set. Boeing's engineers used "unknown unknowns" / "unk-unks" decades before the phrase entered public discourse precisely because aircraft design is a large-world problem - the only way to learn what a metal tube does at 500 mph and 35,000 feet is to fly it.

## Evidence

Kay & King, Ch. 7. Savage's small-world/large-world distinction from *The Foundations of Statistics* (1954), with Savage's own warning against applying the axioms outside the small world. Engineering cases: Boeing's "unknown unknowns / unk-unks" terminology, predating Rumsfeld's 2002 remark by roughly 50 years; the de Havilland Comet's square windows, whose fatigue-cracking failure mode was an unk-unk not on any pre-flight option list. Quote: "the only way to resolve the mystery of what happens to a metal tube flying at 500 mph at 35,000 feet is to try it." These are illustrative cases supporting a conceptual claim, not a statistical result.

nodes.id: Books/radical-uncertainty-kay-king/The Viniar Problem - In-Model Probability Is Not Real-World Probability.md
nodes.title: The Viniar Problem - In-Model Probability Is Not Real-World Probability
nodes.content:
# The Viniar Problem - In-Model Probability Is Not Real-World Probability

**Source**: John Kay & Mervyn King, *Radical Uncertainty: Decision-Making Beyond the Numbers* (W. W. Norton, 2020) - Ch. 19
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/radical-uncertainty-kay-king

## Core Insight

A probability calculated inside a model is conditional on the model being true. To make a claim about probability in the real world, you must compound the in-model probability with the (usually unknowable) probability that the model itself is true. Kay & King name this the Viniar problem, after Goldman Sachs CFO David Viniar, who in August 2007 described markets experiencing "25-standard-deviation events, several days in a row." A 25-sigma event is so improbable that the universe has not existed long enough to expect a single one. So such a report is overwhelmingly more likely to be evidence of model failure than of genuine extreme bad luck.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters

The error is a category confusion between a conditional probability and an unconditional one. Inside the model, a 25-sigma move is near-impossible by construction. But that figure is P(event | model is true), and it tells you almost nothing about P(event) in the world. When you observe an outcome the model deems all but impossible, Bayesian reasoning says the rational update is overwhelmingly toward "the model is wrong," not toward "an astronomically rare thing just happened." Viniar inverted this: he treated the model's verdict as a window onto reality rather than as conditional on the model. The lesson generalises far beyond finance: any reported probability of an extreme event is really a joint statement about the event and the model, and the second term - model failure - usually dominates the first whenever the event is extreme. This is why catastrophic tail outcomes are almost always model failures rather than realised tail draws.

## Evidence

Kay & King: Viniar "confused a probability as calculated within a model with a probability in the world of which the model claimed to be a representation... To make a statement about probability in a real world it is necessary to compound the probability derived from the model itself with the probability that the model is itself true... We shall call this problem of model failure the Viniar problem." The arithmetic of 25 standard deviations supplies the quantitative force: the implied frequency is so low that the multi-billion-year age of the universe is too short to expect one occurrence, let alone several on consecutive days.

nodes.id: Books/radical-uncertainty-kay-king/There Is No Bayesian Dial - Rational Agents Decline Most Bets.md
nodes.title: There Is No Bayesian Dial - Rational Agents Decline Most Bets
nodes.content:
# There Is No Bayesian Dial - Rational Agents Decline Most Bets

**Source**: John Kay & Mervyn King, *Radical Uncertainty: Decision-Making Beyond the Numbers* (W. W. Norton, 2020) - Ch. 22
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/radical-uncertainty-kay-king

## Core Insight

Real decision-makers do not carry a consistent set of subjective probabilities over all events that they read off like a dial whenever asked to bet. Rational behaviour under radical uncertainty is frequently to REFUSE to bet - to decline participation when information is imperfect or asymmetric - which is the opposite of the Ramsey / de Finetti "Dutch book" model that infers a probability from every willingness to wager. People act only once they have built sufficient confidence in a narrative, not because an internal probability crossed a threshold.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters

The Dutch-book construction treats willingness to bet at given odds as revealing a coherent subjective probability, and assumes such a probability exists for every proposition. Kay & King invert the logic: there is no Bayesian dial to read, because for most real propositions people simply have no probability at all - and the rational response to that absence is abstention, not the manufacture of a number. When information is imperfect and may differ from what a counterparty holds, accepting a bet exposes you to adverse selection; declining is the calibrated move.

This reframes "rationality" away from always having a price and toward knowing when to have no opinion. Decision follows narrative confidence, not probabilistic threshold: agents act when "what is going on here" has cohered enough to warrant action, and otherwise wait. Held against the indifference-gamble construction of utility, this is a PRODUCTIVE TENSION - that construction assumes the agent always has a coherent indifference point to elicit, whereas Kay & King argue the honest agent often has none and rightly refuses to be drawn.

## Evidence

Kay & King, Ch. 22. "They do not act until they have sufficient confidence in the narrative they have constructed. There is no Bayesian dial." And: "Rational people will decline to participate in any proposed wager when their information is imperfect and may differ from that held by other people." Illustrated by Warren Buffett's no-called-strikes image of investing: "you never have to swing... All day you wait for the pitch you like." These are argument and illustrative cases, not statistical results.

nodes.id: Books/radical-uncertainty-kay-king/True Black Swans Are Unimaginable Not Low-Probability.md
nodes.title: True Black Swans Are Unimaginable Not Low-Probability
nodes.content:
# True Black Swans Are Unimaginable Not Low-Probability

**Source**: John Kay & Mervyn King, *Radical Uncertainty: Decision-Making Beyond the Numbers* (W. W. Norton, 2020) - Ch. 11 / 14, "Unknown unknowns"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/radical-uncertainty-kay-king

## Core Insight

A genuine unknown unknown is not a tail event on a known distribution but a state of the world you cannot CONCEIVE - and to which, therefore, no probability however small can be coherently attached. Kay & King's distinct refinement of Taleb: the defining property is unconceivability, not rarity. The probability question is not merely hard but incoherent for the genuinely unimaginable - "to identify a probability of inventing the wheel is to invent the wheel."

## Mechanism / Why It Matters

The low-probability framing smuggles in the very thing that is absent: a specified outcome space over which a small number can be defined. But if you could enumerate the outcome and assign it 0.001, you would already have conceived it - and it would no longer be an unknown unknown. The wheel example makes this exact: to state the probability of the invention you must already hold its concept, which is the invention. This collapses any attempt to "fat-tail" your way to safety against true black swans - you cannot reserve probability mass for states you cannot describe. The practical consequence is that robustness and resilience, not probabilistic provisioning, are the only defences against the genuinely unimaginable.

## Evidence

Kay & King: "the observation of a black swan was not a low-probability event; it was an unimaginable event"; and on the conceivability collapse, "to identify a probability of inventing the wheel is to invent the wheel." The argument is conceptual - a critique of the category, demonstrating that probability is undefined where the outcome space cannot be specified - rather than an empirical claim about frequencies.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Anchoring Has Two Mechanisms and a Measurable Index.md
nodes.title: Anchoring Has Two Mechanisms and a Measurable Index
nodes.content:
# Anchoring Has Two Mechanisms and a Measurable Index

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part II, "Anchors"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Anchoring is not one effect but two distinct processes converging on the same outcome: a deliberate System-2 adjustment that stops too soon at the near edge of the plausible range, and an automatic System-1 priming in which associative memory selectively recruits anchor-compatible evidence. It is also one of the few biases that is quantifiable.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
The deeper point, beyond the parent note's "brain reacts to relative changes": anchors are not treated as evidence at all. Random, uninformative anchors move judgments as strongly as plausibly informative ones - so the effect is associative contamination plus lazy adjustment, not Bayesian updating. This makes anchoring exploitable in any negotiation or estimate where a number is spoken first.

## Evidence
- Adjustment mechanism (Epley-Gilovich): head-shaking moves estimates farther from the anchor; cognitive load reduces adjustment.
- Priming mechanism (Mussweiler-Strack): a high-temperature anchor for Germany primed summer words; high car-price anchors primed luxury brands.
- Anchoring index = estimate-difference / anchor-difference, typically ~55%. Redwood-height study: anchors 1,200 vs 180 ft produced estimates 844 vs 282 ft (index 55%). Experienced German judges rolling loaded dice gave 8 vs 5 month sentences (index 50%). Real-estate agents were anchored 41% by an asking price they swore they ignored.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Attribute Substitution Runs on a Mental Shotgun and Intensity Matching.md
nodes.title: Attribute Substitution Runs on a Mental Shotgun and Intensity Matching
nodes.content:
# Attribute Substitution Runs on a Mental Shotgun and Intensity Matching
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part 1, Answering an Easier Question
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
This DEEPENS Attribute Substitution by supplying the HOW the existing note lacks. System 1 answers a hard question by substituting an easier one, and the machinery that makes this possible is two-part: (a) the mental shotgun - System 1 computes far more than was asked - plus (b) intensity matching - it can map any intensity onto any other scale. Together they manufacture an off-the-shelf answer to a question you did not actually understand. This is the engine under most heuristics.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
The shotgun produces an abundance of unrequested computations, one of which is an easy proxy for the hard target question. Intensity matching then translates that proxy's strength onto the scale the hard question demanded. The transferable error: a fluent, confident answer can be the output of this swap rather than of any engagement with the actual question - so the answer's quality is unrelated to the question's difficulty.

## Evidence
Rhyme judgment is slowed when spelling mismatches (VOTE/GOAT) - an unrequested spelling computation intrudes on a sound task, demonstrating the mental shotgun. People fluently translate "Julie read fluently at age 4" into a matching adult height (around 7 feet), a matching GPA, or a matching crime severity, and they agree with one another across a culture - demonstrating intensity matching across arbitrary scales.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Availability Cascades and Probability Neglect Amplify Rare Risks.md
nodes.title: Availability Cascades and Probability Neglect Amplify Rare Risks
nodes.content:
# Availability Cascades and Probability Neglect Amplify Rare Risks

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part II, "Availability, Emotion, and Risk"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
A self-sustaining media-public-policy feedback loop - an "availability cascade" (Kuran-Sunstein) - can inflate a trivial risk into a national priority. It is powered by "probability neglect": the mind handles small probabilities as all-or-nothing, vividly imagining the numerator (the catastrophe) while ignoring the denominator (how rarely it happens).

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Minor event -> media report -> public alarm -> the alarm itself becomes the story -> more coverage, accelerated by "availability entrepreneurs" who profit from the fear. Because probability neglect strips out the base rate, the loop has no internal brake proportional to actual danger. This is the structural reason resource allocation and public fear systematically detach from statistical risk.

## Evidence
- Love Canal and the 1989 Alar-on-apples scare (Meryl Streep's testimony); the net public-health effect of the Alar panic was likely NEGATIVE as people ate fewer apples.
- Terrorism as the paradigm case: in Israel, weekly terror deaths rarely approached traffic deaths, yet dominate fear and policy.
- Debate flag: Slovic vs Sunstein on whether experts or the public should set risk priorities - democratic legitimacy versus calibrated cost-benefit.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Broad Framing and Risk Policies Neutralize Loss Aversion.md
nodes.title: Broad Framing and Risk Policies Neutralize Loss Aversion
nodes.content:
# Broad Framing and Risk Policies Neutralize Loss Aversion

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part IV, "Risk Policies"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Loss aversion is destructive only under NARROW framing. Bundling many small, independent, favorable gambles into one broad frame - a "risk policy" - shrinks the probability of a net loss and neutralizes loss aversion's tax. The practical rule: adopt risk policies, think like a trader, and check outcomes less often.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Narrow framing evaluates each bet in isolation, so each one triggers loss aversion separately. Aggregating independent favorable bets lets the law of large numbers do its work: the ensemble has a tiny chance of net loss even when any single bet feels frightening. Less-frequent monitoring removes the emotional triggering that narrow framing supplies.

## Evidence
Decisions i/ii: 73% chose the dominated combination under narrow framing, while only 3% chose the dominant one - the broad frame makes dominance obvious. Samuelson's friend rejects one 50-50 lose-$100/win-$200 bet yet wants 100 of them (EV +$5,000, only about 1/2,300 chance of any loss). Quarterly rather than daily portfolio checking improves both emotion and returns. Three caveats: gambles must be independent, the loss must not threaten total wealth, and the rule does not apply to genuine long shots.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Causal Base Rates Are Used Statistical Base Rates Are Ignored.md
nodes.title: Causal Base Rates Are Used Statistical Base Rates Are Ignored
nodes.content:
# Causal Base Rates Are Used Statistical Base Rates Are Ignored
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part II, "Causes Trump Statistics"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
The mind incorporates a base rate only when it can be read as a CAUSE acting on the individual case. A merely statistical base rate - a population fact with no causal story - is ignored. The corollary asymmetry: people generalize readily from a single surprising case yet refuse to particularize from a surprising statistic.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
This DEEPENS the prior note that the brain only weighs available, causally legible facts: it specifies the exact filter. Numbers that imply a mechanism operating on the person ("Green drivers are reckless") get used; numbers that are inert background ("85% of cabs are Green") get discarded. The asymmetry explains why vivid anecdotes update beliefs but aggregate statistics leave them untouched.

## Evidence
- Cab problem: the identical 85/15 base rate is ignored when stated as a population fact ("85% of cabs are Green") but used near-correctly when stated causally ("Green cabs cause 85% of accidents").
- Nisbett-Borgida helping study: students taught the shocking statistic (only 4 of 15 helped a seizure victim) learned nothing and predicted the filmed individuals would help anyway, "quietly exempting" them. Students shown two ordinary non-helpers immediately inferred the low general rate.
- Kahneman: "Unwillingness to deduce the particular from the general was matched only by their willingness to infer the general from the particular."

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Cognitive Ease Conflates Truth Familiarity and Good Mood.md
nodes.title: Cognitive Ease Conflates Truth Familiarity and Good Mood
nodes.content:
# Cognitive Ease Conflates Truth Familiarity and Good Mood
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part 1, The Associative Machine / Cognitive Ease
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
System 1 collapses font legibility, repetition, prior exposure, and mood into ONE undifferentiated "ease" signal. Because the inputs are interchangeable and untraceable to their source, fluency produced by ANY cause masquerades as truth. Strain - disfluency - is what wakes System 2 and triggers scrutiny.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
The ease signal is a single readout with no tag for its origin, so the mind cannot tell whether a statement feels right because it is true, because it has been heard before, or because the reader is in a good mood. This guards against treating "it feels true" as evidence: fluency is a confound, not a signal. The corollary is actionable - deliberately inducing strain (harder presentation) recruits System 2 and cuts intuitive errors.

## Evidence
Repeating "the body temperature of a chicken is..." makes an arbitrary completion feel true - illusory truth via mere repetition. Rhyming aphorisms are judged more insightful than identical non-rhyming versions. Pronounceable stock tickers (KAR) outperform unpronounceable ones (PXG) in their first week of trading. A HARD-to-read font cut errors on the bat-and-ball Cognitive Reflection Test from roughly 90% to about 35% by inducing strain that woke System 2.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Decision Weights Diverge from Probabilities - Possibility and Certainty Effects.md
nodes.title: Decision Weights Diverge from Probabilities - Possibility and Certainty Effects
nodes.content:
# Decision Weights Diverge from Probabilities - Possibility and Certainty Effects

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part IV, "The Fourfold Pattern" / "Rare Events"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
People do not weight outcomes by their probabilities. They OVERWEIGHT the move away from impossibility (possibility effect) and the move toward certainty (certainty effect), while compressing the middle range. This is prospect theory's SECOND engine, largely independent of the value function.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Decision weights are psychological responses to probability, not probabilities themselves. There is a further description/experience gap: rare events are overweighted when they are DESCRIBED but underweighted when learned from repeated EXPERIENCE, because most people simply never sample the rare event. This explains both lottery appeal and systemic blindness to tail risk.

## Evidence
Measured decision weights: a 2% chance carries weight ~8.1 (overweighted roughly fourfold); moving from 100% to 98% certainty costs about 13% of subjective value. The Allais paradox: leading economists - Samuelson, Arrow, Friedman, Savage - reversed preference between a 61-vs-63% choice and a 98-vs-100% choice, the certainty effect violating expected-utility axioms. Experienced-vs-described: no banker in 2007 had lived through a devastating crisis, so the rare event was effectively ignored.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Decorrelate Errors - Independent Judgments Beat Correlated Ones.md
nodes.title: Decorrelate Errors - Independent Judgments Beat Correlated Ones
nodes.content:
# Decorrelate Errors - Independent Judgments Beat Correlated Ones
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part 3 / judgment-aggregation discussions
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
The wisdom of crowds works ONLY when individual errors are independent. Any shared influence - open discussion, sequence effects, first-speaker dominance - correlates errors and destroys the aggregation benefit. To extract real information from multiple sources, you must actively FORCE their independence before combining them.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Averaging cancels error only if errors point in random directions. The moment judges influence one another, their errors align, and the average inherits the shared bias instead of cancelling it - more judges then add false confidence rather than accuracy. The actionable rule: collect judgments independently first, aggregate second. The transferable error this guards against is mistaking a confident consensus for many independent confirmations when it is really one opinion echoed.

## Evidence
Kahneman's grading reform: scoring exams question-by-question across all booklets, instead of grading each booklet whole, broke his own halo effect and revealed the true variance in students' grades. Police keep eyewitnesses apart precisely to preserve error independence. The meeting rule: have everyone write down their position BEFORE discussion begins, because open discussion overweights whoever speaks first or loudest and silently correlates the room.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Life as a Story - A Bad Ending Ruins a Good Life.md
nodes.title: Life as a Story - A Bad Ending Ruins a Good Life
nodes.content:
# Life as a Story - A Bad Ending Ruins a Good Life
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part V
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Duration neglect and the peak-end rule scale up from minutes to entire lifetimes. The remembering self represents a whole life as a prototypical slice plus its ending - so adding pleasant-but-lesser years can REDUCE the judged quality of an otherwise-excellent life. This is a less-is-more effect over a lifespan: we care about the narrative ARC of a life independent of the total happiness actually experienced within it.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Because the remembering self averages rather than sums, more good years do not reliably make a better-judged life, and weaker good years can dilute the prototype and drag the verdict down. A life is scored like a story, with a privileged ending - which means our intuitions about a "good life" can diverge sharply from any accounting of experienced welfare.

## Evidence
Diener "Jen" studies. Between-subjects: doubling the duration of Jen's happy life (30 vs 60 years) had NO effect on judged total happiness - duration neglect over a lifetime. Adding 5 "slightly happy" years onto a very happy life caused a SUBSTANTIAL DROP in judged total happiness. Within-subjects, the intuition that the lesser extra years made the whole life worse was, Kahneman reports, "overwhelming."

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Mental Accounting Errors - Sunk Cost and the Disposition Effect.md
nodes.title: Mental Accounting Errors - Sunk Cost and the Disposition Effect
nodes.content:
# Mental Accounting Errors - Sunk Cost and the Disposition Effect

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part IV, "Keeping Score"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Treating outcomes in separate "mental accounts" - each demanded to close at a gain - produces two systematic errors: escalating commitment to losers (the sunk-cost fallacy) and selling winners while holding losers (the disposition effect).

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Closing an account at a loss inflicts the disproportionate pain of loss aversion, so people defer it - holding losers and escalating into failing projects to avoid booking the loss. The desire to close each account in the black overrides the correct, account-blind question of where future money is best placed.

## Evidence
Investors show a documented "massive preference for selling winners over losers"; the after-tax cost of selling the winner instead of the loser is about 3.4% per year, corrected only in December when tax salience rises. Sunk-cost escalation maps onto the fourfold pattern's high-probability-loss cell. Agency wrinkle: escalation can be rational for the EXECUTIVE (canceling stains the record) even when it is wrong for the firm. Teaching the fallacy works - economics and business students walk away from failing projects more readily.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Mental Effort Has a Measurable Signature and Obeys the Law of Least Effort.md
nodes.title: Mental Effort Has a Measurable Signature and Obeys the Law of Least Effort
nodes.content:
# Mental Effort Has a Measurable Signature and Obeys the Law of Least Effort
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part 1, Effort
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Cognitive effort is a real, metered, costly resource, not a metaphor. Pupil diameter tracks it second-by-second, rising and falling with task demand. Because effort is a genuine cost, people gravitate to the least-demanding path - "laziness is built deep into our nature" - which is why the lazy System 2 so often ratifies System 1 rather than checking it.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Effort behaves like a budgeted physiological cost, so the mind economizes on it by default. This is the deep reason biases persist: correcting System 1 requires spending a resource the system is built to conserve. The transferable error this guards against is assuming that more careful thinking is freely available on demand - it is rationed, and the ration favors the cheap, automatic answer. The metered-cost framing also maps cleanly onto compute budgets in machine reasoning.

## Evidence
Beatty-Kahneman pupillometry: the Add-3 task dilates the pupil by roughly 50% and raises heart rate about 7 beats per minute. Dilation follows an inverted-V, peaking at maximum load and collapsing the instant the subject solves the problem or gives up. When demand exceeds capacity, the pupils STOP dilating rather than expanding further. Both skill and intelligence REDUCE the effort signature for the same task - experts spend less to do the same work.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Negativity Dominance - Bad Is Stronger Than Good.md
nodes.title: Negativity Dominance - Bad Is Stronger Than Good
nodes.content:
# Negativity Dominance - Bad Is Stronger Than Good

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part IV, "Prospect Theory" / "The Endowment Effect"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Loss aversion is one instance of a broad biological asymmetry: negative information, events, and emotions are processed faster, weighted heavier, and resist disconfirmation more than positive ones. Because a goal functions as a reference point, falling short looms larger than exceeding it.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
The asymmetry is wired deep enough to be pre-attentive, which means it shapes judgment before System 2 can intervene. Treating loss aversion as a special case of a general negativity dominance explains why it shows up everywhere from threat perception to relationships to performance against targets - the reference point recasts any shortfall as a loss.

## Evidence
The amygdala responds to masked threatening faces shown under 2/100 of a second, below awareness. Bad words (war, crime) capture attention faster than good words. Rozin's demonstration: a cockroach touching a bowl of cherries ruins them, but a cherry does nothing for a bowl of roaches. Gottman found stable marriages need roughly a 5:1 ratio of good to bad interactions. Goals as reference: Pope and Schweitzer analyzed over 2.5 million golf putts - players sink par putts (avoiding a bogey, framed as a loss) about 3.6% more often than birdie putts (a foregone gain).

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Optimistic Bias Is the Engine of Capitalism and a Mispricing of Risk.md
nodes.title: Optimistic Bias Is the Engine of Capitalism and a Mispricing of Risk
nodes.content:
# Optimistic Bias Is the Engine of Capitalism and a Mispricing of Risk

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part III, "The Engine of Capitalism"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Optimistic overconfidence drives the entrepreneurs, inventors, and leaders who disproportionately shape outcomes. Risk-takers mostly UNDERESTIMATE the odds rather than truly loving risk ("bold forecasts and timid decisions"). The same bias is simultaneously the source of economic dynamism and of predictable individual loss - an ensemble-versus-individual split. Competition neglect compounds it. The premortem is a partial remedy.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
At the population level, a steady supply of overconfident risk-takers seeds the rare successes that drive growth, even though most individuals lose. The system harvests value from the failures of "optimistic martyrs," so what is irrational for the individual is productive for the ensemble. Competition neglect - planning as if rivals do not exist - amplifies the error precisely where many optimists converge on the same opportunity.

## Evidence
US 5-year small-business survival is about 35%; entrepreneurs estimated 60% for "any business like yours" but 81% put their OWN odds at 7/10 or higher and 33% said zero chance of failure. Astebro: of inventions graded D/E (predicting failure for over 70%), 47% persisted after being told it was hopeless and on average doubled their losses. Malmendier-Tate: optimistic CEOs overpay and make value-destroying mergers, and award-winning CEOs subsequently underperform. Camerer-Lovallo "competition neglect" (the Disney chairman on same-weekend blockbusters).

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Preferences Are Frame-Bound Not Reality-Bound.md
nodes.title: Preferences Are Frame-Bound Not Reality-Bound
nodes.content:
# Preferences Are Frame-Bound Not Reality-Bound

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part IV, "Frames and Reality"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Because System 1 reacts to descriptions rather than to states of the world, logically equivalent formulations of the same problem systematically reverse preferences - including moral and life-or-death choices. There is often no underlying "true" preference being distorted; the preference is ABOUT the frame. The broader, more inclusive frame is usually the more rational one.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
A frame is not a lens over a pre-existing preference - frequently it constitutes the preference, because System 1 has no access to the invariant state behind a description. This dissolves the notion of a stable utility function waiting to be measured and makes default-setting and description a form of power over choice.

## Evidence
"90% survival" vs "10% mortality": 84% of PHYSICIANS chose surgery under the survival framing versus 50% under the mortality framing - expertise is no defense. The Asian disease problem: risk-averse for "200 saved", risk-seeking for "400 die", same outcomes. Organ-donation opt-out vs opt-in: roughly 86-100% consent (Sweden, Austria) versus 4-12% (Denmark, Germany), driven by the default plus System-2 laziness. MPG illusion: 12->14 mpg saves more fuel than 30->40 mpg, since gallons-per-mile is the better frame.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Regression to the Mean Has an Explanation but No Cause.md
nodes.title: Regression to the Mean Has an Explanation but No Cause
nodes.content:
# Regression to the Mean Has an Explanation but No Cause
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part II, "Regression to the Mean"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Whenever a correlation is less than perfect, extreme scores are followed by more average ones for PURELY statistical reasons - luck does not repeat. Regression has an explanation but no cause. Because the mind demands causation, it manufactures spurious causal stories, and this systematically INVERTS the feedback we receive about reward versus punishment.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
This DEEPENS the static definition of regression by adding three things: the no-cause-but-an-explanation asymmetry, the feedback-inversion it produces, and the control-group rationale that follows. Any performance = skill + luck; the lucky component cannot persist, so extremes regress regardless of intervention. The mind then misattributes the regression to whatever it did in between.

## Evidence
- Israeli Air Force instructors concluded punishment works and praise backfires: they praised after unusually good (lucky) flights that then regressed down, and screamed after unusually bad ones that regressed up.
- A coin-toss-at-target demo reproduced "improve after bad / deteriorate after good" with ZERO feedback.
- Galton (1886) and the best statisticians of the era needed years to grasp it.
- Depressed children "improve" on any treatment by regression alone - which is WHY control and placebo groups exist.
- General implication: "we are statistically rewarded for being nasty and punished for being nice."

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Representativeness Substitutes Similarity for Probability.md
nodes.title: Representativeness Substitutes Similarity for Probability
nodes.content:
# Representativeness Substitutes Similarity for Probability

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part II, "Representativeness"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Asked for a probability, the mind silently answers an easier question - how similar is this case to a prototype or stereotype. This single substitution commits TWO sins at once: it neglects base rates, and it treats worthless evidence as if it were diagnostic (WYSIATI). The fix is also twofold: anchor on a plausible base rate, then interrogate the diagnosticity of your evidence.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
This note's contribution is representativeness AS the substitution mechanism - not base-rate neglect in general, which existing core notes already cover. The second, often-overlooked sin is the evidence-quality failure: people use a description even after being told it is uninformative. Because the failure is usually laziness rather than ignorance, forcing System 2 to engage restores some statistical sensitivity.

## Evidence
- Tom W: 114 psychology grad students who KNEW the base rates and were told the description came from tests "of uncertain validity" still ranked graduate fields purely by stereotype-similarity, predicting rare computer science over the populous humanities.
- Even statistician Robyn Dawes, who knew better, guessed "computer scientist."
- "Frowning" (an induced state of System-2 vigilance) restored some base-rate sensitivity - proving the failure is often laziness, not missing knowledge.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Simple Formulas Beat Expert Judgment in Low-Validity Environments.md
nodes.title: Simple Formulas Beat Expert Judgment in Low-Validity Environments
nodes.content:
# Simple Formulas Beat Expert Judgment in Low-Validity Environments

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part III, "Intuitions vs. Formulas"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
A mechanical combination of a few scores matches or beats trained expert judgment in roughly 60% of studies (the rest tie, which still favors the cheaper formula). A major reason is that humans are NOISY: they give different answers to identical inputs, and unreliable judgments cannot be valid. Equal-weight "improper" models often beat optimally-fitted regression because equal weights are not distorted by sampling accidents.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Experts try to be clever, weighting subtle interactions that are mostly noise in the specific sample. A formula applies the same rule every time, so it has zero internal inconsistency. In low-validity environments the signal is weak; consistency alone then beats erratic human cleverness. This reframes "trust the expert" as a question about environment validity, not credentials.

## Evidence
Meehl's clinical-vs-actuarial review (about 200 studies today; roughly 60% favor algorithms, none reliably favor experts). Counselors predicting freshman GPA lost to a formula (high-school grades plus one aptitude test) on 11 of 14 measures. Ashenfelter's 3-variable weather formula predicts Bordeaux wine prices at correlation above 0.90. Noise: radiologists contradict their own normal/abnormal reads about 20% of the time on the same X-ray. Dawes's "robust beauty of improper linear models"; the Apgar score (5 signs scored 0-1-2) saved hundreds of thousands of infants.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Subjective Confidence Tracks Story Coherence Not Evidence Quality.md
nodes.title: Subjective Confidence Tracks Story Coherence Not Evidence Quality
nodes.content:
# Subjective Confidence Tracks Story Coherence Not Evidence Quality
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part 1, A Machine for Jumping to Conclusions
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
This DEEPENS WYSIATI by naming the engine beneath it: confidence is produced by the coherence and cognitive ease of the story System 1 builds, INDEPENDENT of the amount or quality of evidence. Knowing LESS can raise confidence, because sparse facts fit a clean pattern more easily. WYSIATI - "What You See Is All There Is" - means the mind builds the best story from available evidence and never debits for evidence it does not have. Confidence is a feeling, not a diagnostic.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
The associative machine generates coherence by construction, so "the world makes less sense than it seems." Because the mind cannot represent absent information, missing evidence carries zero weight - and a story with fewer competing facts is easier to make seamless. This guards against the load-bearing error of treating felt confidence as a signal of accuracy. It is instead a readout of how smoothly the story assembled.

## Evidence
Tversky's one-sided-evidence study: participants who heard only ONE side of a legal case were MORE confident in their verdict than those who heard both sides, even though they knew they had heard only one side. The Bananas/Vomit demonstration: a single phrase cascades into an embodied, causally linked story the words never stated - "the coherence comes mostly from the way your mind works," not from the input.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/System 1 and System 2 Are Useful Fictions Not Brain Systems.md
nodes.title: System 1 and System 2 Are Useful Fictions Not Brain Systems
nodes.content:
# System 1 and System 2 Are Useful Fictions Not Brain Systems
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part 1, Two Systems
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
This DEEPENS System 1 by exposing what the two-systems split actually is: a deliberate agent-metaphor, a "psychodrama with two characters," not a claim about brain anatomy. Kahneman adopts the fiction because the mind comprehends agent-with-traits descriptions far more cheaply than property descriptions. System 1 is fast, automatic, effortless, and associative; System 2 is slow, effortful, and deliberate but constitutionally lazy.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
The justification is pure cognitive economy. A sentence about an agent ("the butler steals the cash") is processed more easily than an equivalent sentence about a property, so Kahneman packages a distributed set of processes into two characters to make the model thinkable. The transferable error this guards against is reification: treating a chosen explanatory fiction as a literal mechanism. The AI field has done exactly this, importing "System 1 vs System 2" as if it named two real engines to be built, when Kahneman explicitly disowned the literal reading.

## Evidence
Kahneman states the two systems "are fictitious characters... not systems in the standard sense of entities with interacting aspects or parts," and that "there is no one part of the brain that either of the systems would call home." He defends the device on grounds of comprehension cost: agent-based sentences are processed more fluently than property-based ones, so the fiction earns its place by making the psychology easier to reason about, not by being true.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Taming Intuitive Predictions - Regress Toward the Mean by the Correlation.md
nodes.title: Taming Intuitive Predictions - Regress Toward the Mean by the Correlation
nodes.content:
# Taming Intuitive Predictions - Regress Toward the Mean by the Correlation
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part II, "Intuitive Predictions"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
System 1 matches the extremeness of a prediction to the extremeness of the evidence - prediction equals evaluation - ignoring that weak evidence warrants regression toward the mean. Intuitive predictions are therefore systematically non-regressive and overconfident. The corrective: move from the baseline toward your intuition only by the FRACTION equal to the evidence-outcome correlation.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Because System 1 substitutes "how impressive is the evidence?" for "how predictive is it?", it assigns the same percentile to the predictor and the outcome. Calibrated prediction requires explicitly discounting for the imperfect correlation between them. This is the actionable counterpart to the fact that regression has no cause: you must build the regression in by hand.

## Evidence
- "Julie read fluently at age 4 -> her GPA?" yields ~3.7-3.8 because people assign the same percentile to reading precocity and GPA.
- IDF officer-school study: the distribution of PREDICTED grades was nearly identical to the distribution of EVALUATION grades - completely nonregressive.
- Four-step fix: (1) start at the average / base rate, (2) get the intuitive matching estimate, (3) estimate the evidence-outcome correlation, (4) move that fraction of the distance from average toward intuition.
- Honest cost: an unbiased prediction can never "call" a rare extreme.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/The Affect Heuristic - Feeling Precedes and Generates Belief.md
nodes.title: The Affect Heuristic - Feeling Precedes and Generates Belief
nodes.content:
# The Affect Heuristic - Feeling Precedes and Generates Belief
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part 2, The Science of Availability / Slovic's affect heuristic
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Where emotion is involved, like or dislike comes FIRST, and beliefs about facts - risks, benefits, evidence - are recruited afterward to justify the feeling. System 2 acts as "an apologist for the emotions of System 1, an endorser rather than an enforcer." The order of operations is the bias: the conclusion is felt, then the reasons are assembled.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
A single affective stance toward a thing drives every factual judgment about it, which is why those judgments become improperly coupled. The transferable error: people experience their fact-beliefs as the cause of their attitude when the attitude is actually the cause of the beliefs. This is the substitution of "how do I feel about it?" for "what do I think about it?" - and it explains why presenting one-sided emotional information shifts supposedly independent risk and benefit estimates together.

## Evidence
Slovic's affect heuristic: telling people that an activity's RISK is lower also raises their estimate of its BENEFITS, even when no benefit information is given - one feeling drives both judgments, producing an implausible negative correlation between perceived risk and benefit. Reading a passage extolling only the benefits of a technology LOWERED people's perceived risk of it. Haidt's summary captures the order: "the emotional tail wags the rational dog."

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/The Availability Heuristic - Ease of Retrieval Can Override Count.md
nodes.title: The Availability Heuristic - Ease of Retrieval Can Override Count
nodes.content:
# The Availability Heuristic - Ease of Retrieval Can Override Count

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part II, "The Science of Availability"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
People judge frequency by how fluently instances come to mind rather than by counting them. The sharper finding, beyond "unusual cases feel more frequent": the experienced EASE of retrieval can override the NUMBER of instances retrieved - the feeling of fluency, not the tally, drives the judgment.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Availability is itself an instance of attribute substitution: the hard target ("how frequent / how true of me?") is answered with the easy felt-quality "how smoothly did examples come?" Crucially, the cue is metacognitive, so it can be unmasked. When the difficulty of retrieval is given a spurious external cause, the brain stops reading fluency as evidence - revealing the heuristic as misattributed "unexplained unavailability."

## Evidence
- Schwarz et al.: people asked to list 12 instances of their own assertiveness rated themselves LESS assertive than those who listed only 6 - the last few items were hard to produce, and that difficulty was read as "I'm not very assertive." Fluency beat count.
- The effect VANISHES when the difficulty is attributed to an external cause (background music said to impair retrieval), so the brain no longer treats the strain as self-relevant evidence.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/The Conjunction Fallacy - A Coherent Story Beats Logical Probability.md
nodes.title: The Conjunction Fallacy - A Coherent Story Beats Logical Probability
nodes.content:
# The Conjunction Fallacy - A Coherent Story Beats Logical Probability
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part II, "Linda: Less is More"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
People judge a more detailed, more plausible scenario as MORE probable than the single broader event that strictly contains it. This violates the logical rule that adding a conjunct can only lower probability. System 1 substitutes coherence and plausibility for probability - and it AVERAGES across details rather than summing them. Hence "less is more": adding a vivid condition raises felt likelihood while lowering actual likelihood.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
A representative, story-like description fires System 1's coherence detector. Because the judgment runs on average plausibility rather than set logic, a richer story scores higher than its own superset. For forecasters this is lethal: every added detail makes a scenario feel more credible while making it strictly less probable.

## Evidence
- Linda: 85-89% of undergraduates - and 85% of Stanford decision-science doctoral students trained in probability - rated "bank teller AND active feminist" as more probable than "bank teller."
- Hsee dinnerware: in single evaluation a 24-piece intact set was priced higher ($33) than a 40-piece set containing it plus 7 extra intact pieces ($23), because broken pieces dragged down the AVERAGE.
- A detailed "California earthquake causes a flood" scenario was rated more probable than the broader "a flood somewhere in North America."
- Frequency framing ("how many of 100?") cut errors from 65% to 25%.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/The Experiencing Self and the Remembering Self Have Conflicting Interests.md
nodes.title: The Experiencing Self and the Remembering Self Have Conflicting Interests
nodes.content:
# The Experiencing Self and the Remembering Self Have Conflicting Interests
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part V
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
A person is two distinct entities. The experiencing self lives each moment and answers "Does it hurt now?" The remembering self keeps score, writes the story, and answers "How was it, on the whole?" Crucially, only the remembering self has a voice and makes decisions - so we systematically optimize for the quality of future MEMORIES rather than future EXPERIENCE. Kahneman calls this "the tyranny of the remembering self." (This is a separate split from System 1 / System 2.)

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
The two selves have genuinely conflicting interests: more experienced suffering can buy a better-remembered episode, and the remembering self will choose it. Because decisions are made by memory, not by lived experience, our choices can betray the welfare of the self that actually does the living. This reframes well-being: maximizing remembered satisfaction is not the same as maximizing experienced happiness.

## Evidence
Cold-hand (cold-pressor) experiment: a short trial (60s at 14C) vs a long trial (60s at 14C plus 30s warming to ~15C). The long trial delivers strictly MORE total pain, yet 80% of subjects who felt the pain ease at the end chose to REPEAT the longer trial. They knew which lasted longer and did not use that fact. "Memories are all we get to keep... the experiencing self does not have a voice."

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/The Focusing Illusion - Nothing Is as Important as When You Think About It.md
nodes.title: The Focusing Illusion - Nothing Is as Important as When You Think About It
nodes.content:
# The Focusing Illusion - Nothing Is as Important as When You Think About It
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part V
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Any aspect of life you direct attention to looms disproportionately large in a global judgment, because the act of evaluating it forces you to think about it. But in lived experience you rarely attend to that aspect, so its true contribution to your well-being is small. The focusing illusion is WYSIATI applied to well-being, and its underlying error is duration neglect - ignoring how RARELY you actually think about the thing.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Adaptation is, in large part, the withdrawal of attention: most life circumstances are "part-time states" that affect mood only while in focus. This means major life changes (income, climate, even disability) shape happiness far less than predicted, because we forecast as if they were always in mind when in fact they seldom are.

## Evidence
California study (Schkade-Kahneman): CA and Midwest students had IDENTICAL life satisfaction, yet both wrongly believed Californians are happier - the error traced to over-weighting climate, which residents almost never attend to. The "car" demonstration: you enjoy your car only WHEN you think about it, which is seldom. Paraplegics are in good mood over 50% of the time within a month; observers who KNOW a paraplegic estimate ~41% bad mood, versus 68-70% from people merely imagining the condition.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/The Fourfold Pattern of Risk Attitudes.md
nodes.title: The Fourfold Pattern of Risk Attitudes
nodes.content:
# The Fourfold Pattern of Risk Attitudes

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part IV, "The Fourfold Pattern"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
The interaction of the value function and decision weights produces four predictable risk attitudes: risk-AVERSE for high-probability gains and low-probability losses; risk-SEEKING for low-probability gains (lotteries) and high-probability losses (desperate gambles). This is why the same person buys both lottery tickets and insurance.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Each cell is the joint output of two forces - the curvature of the value function (concave for gains, convex for losses) and overweighting at the probability extremes. Mapping a real decision to its cell predicts whether a person will lock in, gamble, overpay, or escalate. The high-probability-loss cell (top right) is the dangerous one: it drives people to fight past certain defeat.

## Evidence
The 2x2: high-prob gain -> lock in a sure thing or accept a settlement; low-prob gain -> buy lottery tickets; low-prob loss -> overpay for insurance; high-prob loss -> fight a losing case and throw good money after bad. Guthrie's litigation data: strong-case plaintiffs (risk-averse cell) settle for less than statistical expected value, while frivolous-claim plaintiffs (the lottery cell) extract more than justified. The top-right cell is where losing armies fight past certain defeat.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/The Illusion of Validity - Confidence Survives Disconfirming Feedback.md
nodes.title: The Illusion of Validity - Confidence Survives Disconfirming Feedback
nodes.content:
# The Illusion of Validity - Confidence Survives Disconfirming Feedback

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part III, "The Illusion of Validity"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Subjective confidence is a feeling of internal coherence, not a signal of accuracy. Cognitive illusions of skill are MORE stubborn than visual ones: the feeling and the action persist even after the disconfirming fact is intellectually accepted. The mind does not digest threatening facts. The only valid test of skill is persistence of performance across time.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Confidence is manufactured by the ease and coherence of the story System 1 constructs, so it is decoupled from the world it claims to describe. Knowing the statistics that destroy your credential does not dissolve the feeling of skill, because the illusion is generated by a faster process than the correction. Institutions therefore keep paying for skill that does not exist.

## Evidence
Army leaderless-group test: assessors felt total confidence in their officer predictions even after repeated feedback showed the predictions were "little better than random guesses"; confidence returned in full the next day. Wealth-advisory firm: 25 advisers over 8 years yielded 28 cross-year skill correlations averaging roughly 0.01 (zero); shown this, executives "swept it under the rug." Mutual funds: about 2 of 3 underperform yearly, cross-year correlation near zero.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/The Law of Small Numbers - Small Samples Manufacture Extreme Results.md
nodes.title: The Law of Small Numbers - Small Samples Manufacture Extreme Results
nodes.content:
# The Law of Small Numbers - Small Samples Manufacture Extreme Results

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part II, "The Law of Small Numbers"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Extreme outcomes - both the highest and the lowest - are a mathematical artifact of small sample size, not the fingerprint of any cause. Yet the mind reflexively attaches a causal story to them: it sees randomness as regularity and clustering, and trusts tiny samples far more than the arithmetic warrants.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Small samples vary more, so they over-produce extreme averages at both ends. The same cause cannot produce opposite effects, so when the best AND the worst share a feature, that feature is a sampling artifact, not an explanation. We over-detect causes in noise because false-positive causal vigilance was evolutionarily cheap - mistaking a rustle for a predator costs less than the reverse.

## Evidence
- US kidney-cancer study across 3,141 counties: lowest AND highest incidence counties are both disproportionately small rural counties - pure variance, not lifestyle.
- Urn math: samples of 4 yield homogeneous (all-red/all-white) outcomes ~8x more often than samples of 7 (12.5% vs 1.56%).
- Gates Foundation spent ~$1.7B favoring small schools because small schools were over-represented among the BEST - but they are equally over-represented among the WORST.
- Feller's WWII London-bombing gaps looked deliberate but were statistically typical of a random (Poisson) process.
- The basketball "hot hand" passed all randomness tests (Gilovich-Vallone-Tversky). Caveat: Miller-Sanjurjo (2018) show a small real effect survives a selection-bias correction, so the "pure illusion" verdict is now contested.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/The Outside View and the Planning Fallacy.md
nodes.title: The Outside View and the Planning Fallacy
nodes.content:
# The Outside View and the Planning Fallacy

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part III, "The Outside View"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
Forecasters spontaneously take the INSIDE view (their specific plan and current progress) and ignore the OUTSIDE view (the statistical distribution of outcomes for similar projects), even when they personally hold the reference-class knowledge. So plans cluster unrealistically near the best-case scenario. The remedy is reference-class forecasting: anchor on the base rate of similar projects first, then adjust for case specifics.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
The inside view builds a coherent, detail-rich narrative of THIS project succeeding, and coherence breeds confidence (see availability of one's own plan). It structurally cannot represent the unknown unknowns that sink the reference class, because those are not features of the specific plan. The fix is not more effort on the plan but a deliberate switch of attention to outcomes of the comparison class - a distinct cognitive operation, not a refinement of the inside view.

## Evidence
Kahneman's own curriculum team estimated about 2 years; the expert's reference-class knowledge implied 7-10 years and a 40% failure rate (actual: 8 years, then unused). Scottish Parliament: estimated GBP 40M in 1997, reached GBP 431M by 2004. A 2005 rail study found passenger forecasts overestimated in over 90% of cases (average 106% too high), cost overruns of 45%, and no improvement over 30 years. US kitchen remodels: expected $18,658, paid $38,769. Flyvbjerg operationalized reference-class forecasting.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/The Peak-End Rule and Duration Neglect.md
nodes.title: The Peak-End Rule and Duration Neglect
nodes.content:
# The Peak-End Rule and Duration Neglect
**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part V
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
The remembering self scores an episode by AVERAGING its most extreme moment (peak) and its final moment (end), while assigning essentially ZERO weight to how long the episode lasted. This is duration neglect. The deep cause: System 1 represents a set of moments by a prototype or average, not by a sum - the very same averaging-not-summing operation that drives the Linda conjunction fallacy.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
Because a memory is built from a prototype rather than an integral, total experienced quantity drops out. This opens a practical fork. To reduce REMEMBERED pain, lower the peak and taper the ending. To reduce pain ACTUALLY suffered, finish as fast as possible. The two goals can recommend opposite actions - the operational signature of the two-selves conflict.

## Evidence
Colonoscopy study (Redelmeier-Kahneman, N=154, pain rated every 60s): retrospective "total pain" tracked the average of PEAK and END, not the duration-weighted area under the curve. Patient A (8 min, ended at 7/10) recalled it as WORSE than Patient B (24 min, far more total pain, but ended at 1/10). Rats also show duration neglect - only intensity registers.

nodes.id: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman/Value Lives in Changes from a Reference Point - Bernoulli's Error.md
nodes.title: Value Lives in Changes from a Reference Point - Bernoulli's Error
nodes.content:
# Value Lives in Changes from a Reference Point - Bernoulli's Error

**Source**: Daniel Kahneman, *Thinking, Fast and Slow* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011) - Part IV, "Bernoulli's Errors" / "Prospect Theory"
**Document Type**: Book
**Extracted By**: AI (document-insight-extractor, Book Mode)
**Extraction Date**: 2026-06-28
**Scope**: Books/thinking-fast-and-slow-kahneman

## Core Insight
The carrier of subjective value is the CHANGE - a gain or loss relative to a reference point - not the absolute end-state of wealth. This is the variable Bernoulli's 300-year-old expected-utility theory omitted, and the omission is why utility theory fails to describe real choice.

## Mechanism / Why It Matters
The prospect-theory value function packs three features into one S-shaped curve: (1) reference-dependence - value is measured from a baseline, not from zero wealth; (2) diminishing sensitivity - the curve is concave for gains and convex for losses, which mechanically produces risk-seeking in the domain of losses; and (3) loss aversion - the loss side is steeper than the gain side. Fixing the reference point is therefore the master move from which the rest of the architecture follows.

## Evidence
Jack and Jill both end at $5M (Jack rose from $1M, Jill fell from $9M). Utility theory predicts identical happiness; Jack is elated, Jill miserable. The three-bowls-of-water demo shows reference-dependence directly. Diminishing sensitivity: $100->$200 feels larger than $900->$1,000, the same logarithmic compression Fechner found for brightness and loudness. Markowitz proposed reference-dependence ~25 years earlier and was ignored.

nodes.id: 02-Permanent/Knowledge Graphs Make Connections Visible.md
nodes.title: Knowledge Graphs Make Connections Visible
nodes.content:
# Knowledge Graphs Make Connections Visible

A knowledge graph represents notes as nodes and the links between them as edges, turning a collection of ideas into a navigable network. Rendering this network makes relationships explicit that would otherwise stay hidden in linear text.

In a note system, each link between two notes becomes an edge in the graph, and the accumulated edges reveal larger patterns: densely connected hubs that anchor a topic, bridge notes that join otherwise separate clusters, and isolated notes that lack any connection. Seeing this topology helps a reader navigate by association, follow a chain of related ideas, and notice gaps where connections are missing. The graph is not a separate artifact but a different view of the same notes, generated automatically from the links already written between them.

**Why this matters**: It turns the implicit web of links in a note collection into something that can be seen, explored, and analyzed.

**Related concepts**:
- [[Atomic Notes Capture One Idea Each]] - atomic notes are the nodes of the graph
- [[Maps of Content Organize Without Hierarchy]] - a curated, human-made view of part of the graph
- [[Semantic Search Versus Keyword Search]] - graph links offer a third retrieval path beside these two
- [[Spreading Activation Models Associative Recall]] - describes how relevance can spread across graph edges

nodes.id: 02-Permanent/Atomic Notes Capture One Idea Each.md
nodes.title: Atomic Notes Capture One Idea Each
nodes.content:
# Atomic Notes Capture One Idea Each

An atomic note contains a single, self-contained idea expressed in one's own words. Keeping each note focused on one concept makes it easier to link, reuse, and recombine across many contexts.

When a note holds only one idea, it becomes a stable unit of thought that can be referenced from anywhere without dragging unrelated material along with it. Atomicity also forces clarity, because a single idea must be stated plainly enough to stand alone. Over time, a collection of atomic notes behaves like a set of interchangeable building blocks: the same note can support an argument in one project and a contrast in another. This reusability is what distinguishes a knowledge system from a pile of documents, where ideas stay trapped inside the page that first recorded them.

**Why this matters**: Single-idea notes are the smallest reusable unit of knowledge, which is what makes a connected note system possible.

**Related concepts**:
- [[The Zettelkasten Method]] - atomicity is a foundational principle of this method
- [[Knowledge Graphs Make Connections Visible]] - atomic notes become the nodes in such a graph
- [[Progressive Summarization Distills Notes Over Time]] - both aim to compress a note toward its essential idea

nodes.id: 02-Permanent/Progressive Summarization Distills Notes Over Time.md
nodes.title: Progressive Summarization Distills Notes Over Time
nodes.content:
# Progressive Summarization Distills Notes Over Time

Progressive summarization is a technique for compressing notes in layers, highlighting the most important passages on each successive pass. A note is refined gradually so that its essence becomes easy to grasp at a glance later.

The method works in stages: first capture the source material, then on a later review highlight the key sentences, and on a further pass bold or restate the few phrases that matter most. Each layer is added only when the note is revisited, so effort is spent on material that proves useful rather than on everything at once. The goal is to make a note's core idea instantly accessible to a future reader without discarding the fuller context beneath it. Distilling on revisit also deepens engagement, since deciding what to keep requires understanding what the note actually says.

**Why this matters**: It keeps a growing note collection usable by ensuring each note can be understood quickly when it is needed again.

**Related concepts**:
- [[Atomic Notes Capture One Idea Each]] - both drive a note toward a single clear idea
- [[The Collector's Fallacy]] - summarizing is the processing step that mere collecting skips
- [[Spaced Repetition Strengthens Retention]] - each revisit doubles as a chance to review and recall

nodes.id: 02-Permanent/Maps of Content Organize Without Hierarchy.md
nodes.title: Maps of Content Organize Without Hierarchy
nodes.content:
# Maps of Content Organize Without Hierarchy

A Map of Content (MOC) is a note that gathers links to related notes on a theme, acting as a flexible index. It provides structure through curated links rather than by physically nesting files inside folders.

An MOC sits above a cluster of notes and offers an entry point into a topic, listing the notes that matter and briefly describing how they fit together. Because the organization lives in links, a single note can appear in several maps at once, and a map can be reshaped at any time without moving the underlying notes. This makes MOCs well suited to knowledge that does not fit a single tree. As a collection grows, maps can themselves be linked into higher-level maps, producing navigable layers of overview while leaving the notes free to connect in any direction.

**Why this matters**: It shows how a large note collection stays navigable without forcing every idea into one rigid category.

**Related concepts**:
- [[Emergent Structure Beats Predefined Folders]] - maps are how emergent structure is made visible
- [[Atomic Notes Capture One Idea Each]] - maps gather and index atomic notes
- [[Knowledge Graphs Make Connections Visible]] - an MOC is a hand-curated view of part of the graph

nodes.id: 02-Permanent/Spreading Activation Models Associative Recall.md
nodes.title: Spreading Activation Models Associative Recall
nodes.content:
# Spreading Activation Models Associative Recall

Spreading activation is a model of memory in which activating one concept automatically energizes the concepts connected to it. Activation flows outward across a network of associations, with strength diminishing as it travels farther from the starting point.

The model originated in cognitive psychology to explain why recalling one idea makes related ideas easier to retrieve, and it has since informed how networked information systems rank results. When a starting node is activated, energy passes along its edges to neighboring nodes, then to their neighbors, weakening with distance so that closely related items receive the most activation. Applied to a note collection, beginning from one note and spreading activation across its links surfaces a ranked neighborhood of associated notes. This mirrors how human memory retrieves by association rather than by exact address.

**Why this matters**: It provides a principled way to retrieve not just a matching note but its most relevant associated notes.

**Related concepts**:
- [[Knowledge Graphs Make Connections Visible]] - the network across which activation spreads
- [[Semantic Search Versus Keyword Search]] - an alternative, similarity-based route to relevance
- [[Spaced Repetition Strengthens Retention]] - both concern how associative memory is accessed and reinforced

nodes.id: 02-Permanent/Spaced Repetition Strengthens Retention.md
nodes.title: Spaced Repetition Strengthens Retention
nodes.content:
# Spaced Repetition Strengthens Retention

Spaced repetition is a learning technique in which material is reviewed at increasing intervals over time. Reviewing just as a memory begins to fade strengthens it more efficiently than repeated review in a single session.

The technique builds on the spacing effect, the observation that information is retained better when study sessions are distributed rather than massed together. Each successful recall after a delay reinforces the memory and allows the next review to be scheduled further out, so well-learned items are revisited rarely while difficult ones return often. The active effort of retrieval is central: trying to remember, rather than simply rereading, is what produces durable learning. Many study systems automate the scheduling, presenting each item at the moment review is expected to be most effective.

**Why this matters**: It offers an evidence-based way to retain knowledge long term while spending the least review effort.

**Related concepts**:
- [[Spreading Activation Models Associative Recall]] - describes the retrieval process repetition reinforces
- [[The Collector's Fallacy]] - retention is the goal that mere collecting fails to reach
- [[Progressive Summarization Distills Notes Over Time]] - both improve how well material is later recalled

nodes.id: 02-Permanent/The Zettelkasten Method.md
nodes.title: The Zettelkasten Method
nodes.content:
# The Zettelkasten Method
